coredata.dc:title: Modulating photon harvesting through dynamic non-covalent interactions for enhanced photochemical CO2 reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shao-Hai
Qi, Ming-Yu
Fan, Yun-Yan
Yang, Ye
Anpo, Masakazu
Yamada, Yoichi M.A.
Tang, Zi-Rong
Xu, Yi-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Molecular transition metal complexes are widely explored in artificial photosynthetic redox catalysis due to their extraordinary properties. However, these catalysts generally suffer from the intrinsic thresholds of photon harvesting efficiency and spectrum response range, greatly limiting their further applications. Polypyridyl ruthenium (Ru) has been perceived as a paradigmatic photosensitizer in CO2 reduction systems but seldomly maneuvered. Herein, we develop a novel and practicable pseudo-supramolecular assembly at a molecular scale by conjugation with ultra-small nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) light chromophore for enhanced photochemical conversion of CO2 to CO. The reversible self-assembly of NCQDs with Ru is driven by dynamic non-covalent π-conjugated and electrostatic interactions. Femtosecond transient absorption and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra combined with control experiment results collaboratively demonstrate that vectorial photo-induced exciton cascade occurs by means of their unique photophysical properties in the as-assembled NCQDs/Ru dyad, enabling the superior photosensitized CO2 reduction performance in both homogeneous and heterogeneous systems. This work is expected to not only shed new light on the development of promising artificial light-harvesting systems but also promote the implementation of metal complex-based systems for photoredox-catalyzed reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-covalent
Supramolecular
Carbon quantum dots
Energy transfer
CO2 reduction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
Preparation of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO) nanosheets
Dynamic non-covalent interactions and energy transfer between NCQDs and Ru
Catalytic performances of NCQDs/Ru dyad for the CO2 reduction
The extended applications of NCQDs/Ru dyad for efficient CO2 reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General mechanism for TMCs-based photochemical CO2 reduction systems. a) TMCs as single photocatalyst; b) electron transfer (ET) from TMCs as photosensitizer to cocatalyst (TMCs or others); c, d) the introduction of light-harvesting unit (TMCs or others) to improve the photocatalytic/sensitized efficiency of TMCs via energy transfer (EnT) processes.

a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis procedure of NCQDs. b) X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the NCQDs and CQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of c, d) NCQDs and f, g) CQDs. The insets: corresponding size distributions and high resolution-TEM (HR-TEM) images, respectively. The position of CQDs with low crystallinity is highlighted with a white circle. e, h) Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of NCQDs and CQDs, respectively. The insets: corresponding height distributions.

a) Attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared (ATR-FTIR). b) 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR, in D2O) spectra of citric acid, urea, NCQDs, and CQDs, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of NCQDs. c) survey and d–f) N 1s, C 1s, and Na 1s.

a) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCQDs/Ru/TEOA systems in MeCN/H2O with identical volume and AM1.5 standard solar spectrum. b) MALDI-TOF-MS spectra of NCQDs/Ru, CQDs/Ru, and blank Ru, respectively. c) Zeta potentials of NCQDs, CQDs, and the corresponding mixtures passivated with Ru complex. The inset is a schematic diagram of Zeta potential of negatively charged particle. d) 13C NMR spectra of NCQDs, Ru, and the corresponding mixture in D2O, respectively. e) Normalized photoluminescence spectra of NCQDs in aqueous solution under different excitation wavelengths according to the maximum emission intensity and the normalized absorbance spectrum of Ru complex. f) PL spectra of NCQDs with different amounts (mg) of Ru complex in MeCN/H2O/TEOA solvent under identical conditions (excitation wavelength at 400 nm).

a) CO2 photoreduction performance over Ru complex with different amounts of NCQDs. Reaction conditions: Solvent (MeCN:H2O, 3:2, vol/vol, 5 mL); TEOA (1 mL); Ru complex (10 mg); irradiation time 2 h (visible light, λ ≥ 420 nm); CO2 atmosphere (1 atm). b) The control experiments over NCQDs/Ru system under various reaction conditions. 1. CO2 was replaced by Ar. 2. No TEOA. 3. No light irradiation. 4. No Ru complex. 5. No Ru and NCQDs. 6. Ru was replaced by equal molar mass of [Ru(bpy)2]Cl2 or [Ru(phd)3](PF6)2. Inset: the photo of homogenous solutions of Ru, NCQDs, CQDs and corresponding hybrid systems. c) Detected mass spectrum signal of 13CO (m/z = 29) produced over NCQDs/Ru. d) Cyclic voltammograms of NCQDs/Ru measured in acetonitrile solution containing 0.1 M TBABF4. Scan rate: 100 mV s−1. e) The CO2 photoreduction performance over NCQDs/Ru under cutoff light (λ1 ≥ 420 nm) plus tuned monochromatic light irradiation (λ2). f) The CO2 photoreduction performance over pristine NCQDs/Ru under monochromatic light (λ2 = 365 nm) plus tuned cutoff light (λ1 ≥ 420 nm) irradiation. The inset of f) is concise reaction setup model.

a) The Hg2Cl2 molecule as an apparent indicator demonstrating the intermolecular energy transfer between Ru and NCQDs. The inset is the crystal structure of Hg2Cl2. Femtosecond transient absorption spectra of b) NCQDs and c) NCQDs/Ru in MeCN/H2O/TEOA at indicated delay times from 1 ps to 2 ns (pump excitation at 400 nm). d) Time-resolved PL decay kinetics (circles) and fits (solid lines) of emission of the excitons of NCQDs dispersed in MeCN/H2O/TEOA solution without (upper panel) and with (lower panel) Ru complex excited at 375 nm and monitored at 520 nm. e) Schematic illustration for the energy transfer (EnT) mechanism over Ru complex with NCQDs as the antenna under light irradiation and the extended application with designed cocatalyst for the CO2 reduction.

a) XRD patterns and SEM images of b, c) Co(OH)2 precursors and d, e) CoxP nanoplates. f, g) The CO2 photoreduction performance over the NCQDs/Ru system using the CoxP and Co(bpy)3 2+ complex (denoted as Co) as cocatalysts under different measurement conditions (Table S2), respectively. h) The comparison of CO2 reduction performance of Ru and NCQDs/Ru systems using the homogeneous Co and heterogeneous CoxP as cocatalysts respectively with the previously reported Ru-based cocatalyst systems for CO2 reduction.

head.ce:title: Modulating photon harvesting through dynamic non-covalent interactions for enhanced photochemical CO2 reduction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Graduate School of Engineering, Osaka Prefecture University, Osaka, 599-8531, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: RIKEN Center for Sustainable Resource Science, Hirosawa, Wako, Saitama, 351-0198, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: RIKEN Center for Sustainable Resource Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: RIKEN Center for Sustainable Resource Science, Hirosawa, Wako, Saitama 351-0198, (Japan)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and practicable pseudo-supramolecular assembly at a molecular scale by dynamic conjugation with ultra-small NCQDs as ultraviolet-visible light chromophore to promote the photosensitization efficiency of Ru molecules for photochemical CO2 reduction to CO in both homogeneous and heterogeneous systems.

Molecular transition metal complexes are widely explored in artificial photosynthetic redox catalysis due to their extraordinary properties. However, these catalysts generally suffer from the intrinsic thresholds of photon harvesting efficiency and spectrum response range, greatly limiting their further applications. Polypyridyl ruthenium (Ru) has been perceived as a paradigmatic photosensitizer in CO2 reduction systems but seldomly maneuvered. Herein, we develop a novel and practicable pseudo-supramolecular assembly at a molecular scale by conjugation with ultra-small nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) light chromophore for enhanced photochemical conversion of CO2 to CO. The reversible self-assembly of NCQDs with Ru is driven by dynamic non-covalent π-conjugated and electrostatic interactions. Femtosecond transient absorption and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra combined with control experiment results collaboratively demonstrate that vectorial photo-induced exciton cascade occurs by means of their unique photophysical properties in the as-assembled NCQDs/Ru dyad, enabling the superior photosensitized CO2 reduction performance in both homogeneous and heterogeneous systems. This work is expected to not only shed new light on the development of promising artificial light-harvesting systems but also promote the implementation of metal complex-based systems for photoredox-catalyzed reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Mimicking natural plant photosynthesis to convert solar energy and CO2 into value-added chemicals has been considered as an attractive and sustainable strategy to reduce our dependence on fossil fuels and alleviate global warming [1–3]. Recently, nature-evolved molecular light-harvesting systems with the antennae effect are extensively utilized in this area [4–6]. In particular, transition metal complexes (TMCs) represent an important class of molecular catalysts with favorable photophysical properties and tunable ligand environment towards highly efficient and selective processes for CO2 reduction (Fig. 1 ), where the routes a) and b) have been extensively investigated [7,8]. It is recognized that efficiently harvesting and utilizing the incident photons is one of critical prerequisites towards enhancing the catalytic performance of TMCs light-harvesting systems. Up to date, considerable efforts have been devoted towards enhancing the capacity via covalent coupling strategies, including the hybrids with solid molecular assemblies, such as metal organic frameworks (MOFs) and porous coordination polymers systems and the development of ingenious transition metal-based supramolecular systems integrated light-harvesting antenna with catalytic unit in one species [9–11]. However, these strategies are plagued with some inherent shortcomings such as complex synthetic procedures and low natural abundance. Therefore, it is promising yet challenging to exploit novel systems that are constituted with an efficient and cost-effective functionalized antenna as an alternative.

Generally, two critical requirements for constructing efficient light-harvesting system include that i) photon energy needs to be efficiently captured and transported in the interior of donors, and ii) energy transfer (EnT) process between adjacent donors and acceptors requires the synergy of energy matching and suitable distance, including Förster resonance energy transfer and Dexter energy transfer [12]. To this regard, cost-effective carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as building blocks could offer the promise to meet the above requirements owing to its intrinsic advantages including good water solubility, high photostability, surface modification flexibility, tunable composition, and fluorescent emitting [13]. Specifically, ones exhibit distinctive excitation-dependent or excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) emission features [14,15], which could potentially contribute to realizing the photon switching and energy transfer. On the other hand, non-covalent forces such as electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonding, and van der Waals interactions may be promising ways to realize the convenient self-assembly with TMCs [16]. For instance, small size CQDs possessing high graphitization, akin to graphene fragments at a molecular scale [17], could facilitate the π-stacking interaction with the polycyclic ligands of TMCs [18,19], enforcing the parallel orientation of the dipoles for energy transfer (exciton transfer). In addition, passivation with electron-rich heteroatoms in the structural network of CQDs would also be favorable to the modulation of the intrinsic electronic structure for promoting visible light harvesting and PL quantum yield [20]. To the best of our knowledge, pertinent studies on the application of metal-free nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) for the construction of supramolecular-like artificial light-harvesting systems have been rarely presented.

Herein, we report the advancement of proof-of-concept design of a pseudo-supramolecular assembly involving NCQDs (∼2 nm) as light-harvesting antenna and molecular transition metal complex [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (denoted as Ru) with the integration of light acceptor and photoredox center in one integrated ensemble for CO2 reduction. Spectral characterizations and experimental results verify that the rationally synthesized NCQDs induce effective spatial and dynamic non-covalent interactions with Ru complex without using any complicated synthetic procedures. The optimal NCQDs/Ru dyad exhibits a more than 3-fold increase in the catalytic activity for photochemical reduction of CO2 to CO in a water-containing reaction system compared with pristine Ru complex counterpart, despite its intrinsic reactive inertness (Fig. S1). Impressively, under cutoff visible light irradiation, UV light can be effectively utilized via energy transfer mechanism to further enhance the photon-harvesting efficiency of Ru complex, which is attributed to the photon switching (down-conversion) properties of NCQDs. Furthermore, the general application of such facile cascade assembly-promoted energy transduction effect for efficient homogeneous and heterogeneous CO2 reduction systems has favorably been demonstrated to be feasible, enabling the Ru-based photosensitized systems more cost-effective for practical applications.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %), urea (CH4N2O, 99.0 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96.0 %), acetonitrile (C2H3N, MeCN, 99.0 %), triethanolamine (C6H15O3N, TEOA, 99.0 %), ethanol (C2H6O, 99.7 %), cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, 99.0 %), deuterium oxide (D2O, 99.9 %), and hexamethylene tetramine (C6H12N4, HMT, 99.0 %) were all obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2,2′-bipyridyl (C10H8N2, 99.0 %), cis-Dichlorobis(2,2-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) ([Ru(bpy)2]Cl2, 97 %), Tris(110-phenanthroline-5,6-dione)ruthenium(II) bis(hexafluorophosphate) ([Ru(phd)3](PF6)2, 97 %) and tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate ((C4H9)4N(BF4), TBABF4, 99.0 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. Mercuruy (Hg, 99.9995 % metals basis) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Tris(2,2’-bipyridine) dichlororuthenium (II) hexahydrate ([Ru(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O, 98 %) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) or other companies, including Sigma (99.95 %), Aladdin (98 %), Acros (98 %). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Carbon dioxide (CO2), hydrogen (H2), and argon (Ar) are super grade purity (99.999 %) supplied by Fuzhou Lianzhong Industrial Gases Co., Ltd. CO2 (10 % in CO2/Ar mixture gas) was obtained from Fujian Nanan Chenggong Gas Co., Ltd. and 13CO2 (abundance, 99.7 %) was provided by Sichuan Winntec Specialty Gases Co., Ltd. The deionized water (DI) was used in the whole experimental process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In detail, 20 g of citric acid and 20 g of urea were manually mixed uniformly using a mortar for 30 min. The mixed powders were then thermolyzed in furnace under static air at 180 °C for 40 h. Then the porous solid was firstly dissolved and stirred with DI water and NaOH solution (5 M). More NaOH solution (5 M) was subsequently added to neutralize the acidic NCQDs to pH 7. Finally, the resulting yellow-green aqueous suspension was centrifuged and lyophilized to obtain NCQDs powders. For comparison, the undoped CQDs was synthesized with pure citric acid (40 g) in the absence of urea. [21]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO) nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: GO was first fabricated by a modified Hummers method referenced in previous report. [22] Typically, 40 mg of GO was dispersed with 35 mL of deionized water, and then 12 g of urea was added into the GO dispersion under sonication for 1 h. After that, the solution was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 10 h. The final product was collected by centrifugation, washing, and subsequently lyophilization.

In a typical synthesis, the β-Co(OH)2 nanoplates (NPs) as precursors were synthesized according to a previously reported method with slight modifications. [23] CoCl2.6H2O and hexamethylene tetramine were dissolved in 200 mL of a 9:1 mixture of deionized water and ethanol to give the final concentrations of 10 and 60 mM, respectively. The reaction solution was then heated at 90 °C under magnetic stirring. After being heated for about 1 h, the pink-colored solid product was filtered and washed with deionized water and ethanol several times, and then dried at 60 °C in the air for 12 h. Subsequently, CoxP NPs were synthesized through a simple gas-solid reaction. Typically, NaH2PO2 powder in a porcelain boat was placed at the upstream side of the tubular furnace and another boat loaded with β-Co(OH)2 precursor was placed at the downstream side of the furnace, which are placed apart. The tube was sealed, evacuated, and flushed with high-purity Ar gas to provide oxygen-free environment. Subsequently, the furnace was firstly preheated to 150 °C holding for 30 min and then ramped to 300 °C under Ar atmosphere, at a ramping rate of 5 °C min−1. The sample was held at the setting temperature for 120 min. After the furnace naturally cooled down to room temperature, the as-prepared CoxP material was collected and the color of the sample was changed from pink into black.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were performed on a JEOL Model JEM 2010 EX microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image and elemental mapping were recorded on a FEI Talos F200X STEM operated at 200 kV. A Bruker Dimension Icon system was used to measure the atomic force microscopy (AFM) spectra. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was employed to determine the morphology of the samples on a Hitachi New Generation cold field emission SEM SU-8010 spectrophotometer. The crystal phase properties of the samples were analyzed with a powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance) using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range from 10° to 90° with a scan rate of 0.02° per second at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and XPS-valence spectra were collected using a Thermo Scientific ESCA Lab 250 spectrometer, which was made of a monochromatic Al Kα as the X-ray source, a hemispherical analyzer, and a sample stage with multiaxial adjustability to obtain the surface composition of the samples. All of the binding energies were calibrated by the C1 s peak at 284.6 eV. Attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) was carried out on a Nicolet Nexus iS50 FT-IR spectrometer operating in ATR mode. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were taken using a Bruker AVANCE NEO 600 spectrometer (14.1 T/600 MHz). Zeta-potential (pH = 7) and particle size distribution of dispersions were determined by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano S with samples in DI water held at a constant 25 °C. Each sample was equilibrated for 2 min before measurement. Data are presented as box-plot based on seven independent experiments. In the box-plots, the center line represents the median, the bounds of box stand for the upper and lower quartiles and the small squares represent mean values. The absorption spectra of samples were analyzed on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1750, Shimadzu Co.). The molecular weight of the samples was studied by a matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF-MS, Bruker ultraflextreme) equipped with a pulsed nitrogen laser (337 nm) at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Note: The measured molecular weight may be slightly smaller than the actual value, which is attributed to the effect of laser power required for the mild ionization. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were analyzed on a Hitachi F4600 spectrophotometer. The absolute quantum yields of NCQDs and CQDs were measured on a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba JobinYvon) with a calibration sphere. The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra for samples were analyzed on an Edinburgh Analytical Instrument FLS980 spectrophotometer. Femtosecond transient absorption spectra were conducted using a regeneratively amplified Ti:sapphire laser system (Coherent Astrella, 800 nm, pulse duration ∼35 fs and 1KHz repetition rate). The delay between pump light and probe light was tuned by the delay line (minimum step: 50 fs; maximum delay: ∼5 ns). A pump wavelength of 400 nm was controlled by neutral density filter wheel to the power of 0.788 mW.

Energy-transfer efficiency (ΦET ), the fraction of the absorbed energy that is transferred to the acceptor, was experimentally measured as a ratio of the PL intensities of the donor in the absence and presence of the acceptor (ID and IDA), as shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
Φ ET
= 1-
| DA
| D

Stern-Volmer equation.
(2)
| 0
|
= 1+
k q
τ 0
Q

In the Eq. (2), I0 and I are the PL intensities without and with a quencher, respectively, kq is the apparent rate of quenching, τ0 is the excited-state lifetime without a quencher, and [Q] is the quencher concentration.

The electrochemical tests were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (MUTI AUTOLAB M204) with a conventional three-electrode cell, which uses a Pt counter electrode, glassy carbon (GC) electrode as the working electrode (3 mm diameter), and saturated calomel electrode (SCE, saturated KCl solution) as the reference electrode. The cyclic voltammetry was measured with mild stir at 100 mV s−1 in MeCN solution containing tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (0.1 M) as supporting electrolyte. The electrolyte was purged with saturated Ar or CO2 before cyclic voltammograms measurements.

The photocatalytic CO2 reduction was performed in a 35 mL double-walled quartz reactor with a magnetic stir bar (500 rpm) at atmospheric pressure. The temperature of the reactant solution was maintained at 25 °C by a flow of cooling water (EYELA CCA-112A) during the reaction. A 300 W Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.) equipped with various cutoff filters (420 nm, 510 nm, and 570 nm) and band-pass filters (365 nm, 420 nm, 450 nm, 500 nm, and 650 nm; the wavelength bandwidth is ± 10 nm) was used as the light source. The irradiation area was controlled as 8 cm2 and the incident light power density is 406 mW·cm−2 (λ ≥ 420 nm). Typically, 4 mg NCQDs or CQDs, 10 mg Ru complex, 2.0 mL deionized water, 3.0 mL acetonitrile, and 1.0 mL triethanolamine were added in the quartz reactor. This system was thoroughly degassed with ultrapure CO2 for 30 min to remove the air before the reaction. After reaction for 2 h, gaseous products, carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H2), were measured using gas chromatography (GC-2014C, Shimadzu) with a flame ionization detector (FID) and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). Product gases were calibrated with the standard gas (CO and H2) (Fig. S27). The liquid products such as HCOOH and CH3OH were qualitatively measured by 1H NMR in D2O. Note that there were no detectable liquid products formed in our reaction systems (Fig. S28). Isotope experiment was performed by gas chromatography − mass spectrometry (GC–MS, 7890B and 5977A, Agilent). The selectivity for CO was calculated using the eq. (3) below, in which R (μmol/h) refers to the evolved rate of the gas product (CO or H2) within a certain period under light irradiation.
(3)
Selectivity
C O
%
=
R
CO
R
CO
+ R
H
2
× 100%

The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was measured under the same photocatalytic reaction conditions with irradiation light through a band-pass filter. The photon flux of incident light was measured using an Optical Power/Energy Meter (Newport 842-PE). Depending on the amount of CO produced by the photocatalytic reaction in an average of 1 h, and the AQE was calculated according to the following eq. (4):
(4)
AQY
C O
%
=
Ne Np
× 100% =
2 × M × N A × h × c
S × P × t × λ
× 100%
Where Ne is the amount of reaction electrons, Np is the amount of incident photons, M is the molar amount of CO molecules, NA is Avogadro constant, h is the Planck constant, c is the speed of light, S is the irradiation area, P is the intensity of the irradiation, t is the photoreaction time and λ is the wavelength of the monochromatic light.

The turnover number (TON) and turnover frequency (TOF) related to the cocatalyst were calculated according to Eqs. (5) and (6):
(5)
TON =
n
C O
n
C a t
(6)
TO F
=
TON t
where n CO is the number of moles of produced carbon monoxide, n Cat is the number of moles of catalyst initially present in solution, t is the reaction time (h).

NCQDs with simple accessibility were firstly synthesized from available and renewable resources (citric acid and urea), involving a facile intermolecular condensation reaction in air at 180 °C (Fig. 2 a). During the reaction procedure, driven by external thermal energy, citric acid and passivation agent (urea) were subjected to dehydration and doping during the growth of carbon nuclei. The crystal structures of as-prepared samples were investigated by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 2b, the pattern of NCQDs exhibits a sharp peak centered at about 27° with a small full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) and high signal to noise ratio, corresponding to the typical (002) diffraction of crystalline graphitic carbon. [24] For comparison, the undoped CQDs synthesized using pure citric acid under

identical conditions features a low crystalline structure with a broadening peak around 24.5°, suggesting the carbon material with few graphitic sp2 carbon atoms domains. [15] The introduction of urea reduces the crystallization temperature of citric acid and induces the formation of eutectic during the intermolecular condensation reaction processes. The matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF-MS) reveals that the molecular weight of NCQDs and CQDs is both smaller than 1200 Da (Fig. S2), [25] indicating these obtained quantum dots at macromolecular scale with an inorganic carbon core and organic functional groups as subunits endowing molecular-like behavior. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) were performed to unveil the structures and morphology of as-prepared products. As illustrated in Fig. 2 c-h, the well-dispersed NCQDs and CQDs exhibit uniform morphology with size about 2 nm and 2.4 nm, respectively. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image for NCQDs shows the presence of distinct crystalline carbon domains with the lattice spacing value of 0.249 nm, which is ascribed to the (100) plane of graphite carbon. In contrast, the CQDs are mainly composed of amorphous polymer carbon, which is in conformity with the XRD results. The AFM images and corresponding height statistical distributions indicate the average topographic height of the NCQDs and CQDs are 1.5 nm and 2.5 nm, respectively. These results suggest that the CQDs are quasi-spherical while the NCQDs possess a few-layer quasi-planar graphitized structure.

To scrutinize the pyrolysis processes, the structure and composition of the samples were investigated by attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. Fig. 3
a shows the representative spectra peaks of the main oxygen-containing organic functional groups of NCQDs and CQDs including the bands at about 1370 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1, revealing the presence of C–O and C–O groups on the surface and edge, respectively. [26] Interestingly, the spacing of characteristic peaks of carboxylates broadens after nitrogen doping, which is similar to the previous report [27]. Some emerging characteristic peaks in the spectrum of NCQDs in comparison to that of CQDs, which can be attributed to the inclusion of nitrogen doping, implying the decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions occurred between the precursors (citric acid and urea). 13C NMR spectra further demonstrate the total pyrolysis of precursor molecules and the successful inclusion of nitrogen elements, where numerous emerging carbon environments were observed in the spectrum of NCQDs (Fig. 3b), corresponding to carbonyl and amide carbon (140−170 ppm), graphitic and amorphous sp2 carbon (90−110 ppm), and amorphous sp3 carbon (20−60 ppm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to provide further structural insights. The survey XPS spectrum confirms the presence of C, O, N, and Na elements on the surface of NCQDs (Fig. 3c). After deconvolution, the N 1s spectra can be resolved into three peaks centered at 399.0, 400.2, and 401.2 eV (Fig. 3d), representing pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic type of N atoms doped in the graphitized skeleton, respectively, [28,29] indicating the successful nitrogen doping during the intermolecular condensation reaction processes. For C 1s peaks, it can be seen that five peaks can be deconvoluted, and the peak at 285.4 eV is related to the bonds of C–N for NCQDs (Fig. S3e). The Na 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3f) shows the typical peak observed at 1071.25 eV, indicating the existence of sodium carboxylate owing to the neutralization treatment with NaOH in the preparation process. In contrast, no N 1s signal was detected in the CQDs sample (Fig. S3), further suggesting the successful nitrogen doping during the synthesis processes for NCQDs.The aforementioned results suggest that the NCQDs consist of inorganic graphitic carbon core, defects, and organic groups at their surfaces, providing structural merits to non-covalently interact with the aromatic metal complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic non-covalent interactions and energy transfer between NCQDs and Ru
ce:section.ce:para:
To gain insight into the intermolecular interaction with Ru complex, structural and spectral characterizations were carried out. The photophysical properties of the samples were investigated using the ultraviolet−visible (UV−vis) absorption spectroscopy. The characteristic absorption peaks of NCQDs and Ru complex are appeared at 407 and 453 nm, respectively, while the maximum absorption wavelength of the hybrid shifts to 418 nm (Fig. 4
a and S4). In addition, the absorption spectrum of hybrid system closely overlaps the calculated weighted sum of that of individual component, which is advantageous for photochemical reactions using photon energy located in the visible light spectrum. Meanwhile, no significant spectral red-shifts were observed, suggesting the absence of extended π-conjugated structure between the graphitic NCQDs and bipyridine ligand of Ru complex. Similar phenomena were also observed in different solvents or inverse UV–vis titration plots (Fig. S5), excluding the effects of solvent polarity and pH on the self-assembly. In sharp contrast, similar features were not observed in the spectra of CQDs/Ru system due to the weak absorption of CQDs (Fig. S6). HAADF-STEM image and EDX spectra obtained from a mixture of NCQDs and Ru clearly show that Ru is uniformly dispersed around NCQDs without the formation of aggregations (Fig. S7). MALDI-TOF-MS spectra of both mixture samples show similar main peaks compared to blank Ru (Fig. 4b), indicating the components remain unchanged without the formation of robust bonding interactions. To corroborate whether the weak electrostatic interactions exist between NCQDs and Ru complex, the variation of apparent surface charge of the samples was first monitored by Zeta potential measurements. The charge distribution of CQDs obtained at pH 7 exhibits a lower electronegativity compared to NCQDs (Fig. 4c), suggesting that the surface functional groups as the predominant species lead to the electronegativity. Unexpectedly, with the introduction of Ru complex, the surface charge of quasi-spherical CQDs is slightly increased in comparison with that of NCQDs, indicating that the ruthenium (II) complex can favorably spatially interact with the negative functional groups of NCQDs, [30] which is confirmed by the evolution of 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 4d). Moreover, to interrogate if the electron-rich nitrogen dopants of NCQDs provide coordination sites for immobilizing ruthenium atoms, [31] the surface chemical environment was characterized by in-situ ATR-FTIR and XPS spectroscopy. No apparent spectral changes appeared (Fig. S8 and S9), indicating that electron-rich nitrogen atoms in planar NCQDs could ineffectively interact with ruthenium (II) atom via metal-ligand coordination owing to the surrounded six-coordinated bipyridine ligands. Furthermore, the observable aggregations emerged after increasing the concentration of components, resulted from the enhanced non-covalent interactions, which can be regulated by controlling the dispersity due to the difference of solvophilicity (Fig. S10). Based on the above results, the assembly of Ru complex with NCQDs is driven by the dynamic and reversible π-conjugated and electrostatic interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performances of NCQDs/Ru dyad for the CO2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para: The artificial photocatalytic activity of the systems was investigated in CO2-saturated acetonitrile/water solution with TEOA as a sacrificial electron donor under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). As shown in Fig. 5 a, as a benchmark, pristine Ru complex delivers small amounts of products with a low CO/H2 ratio triggered via metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) mechanism, which is consistent with previous reports. [36] Notably, the performance toward CO2 reduction to CO is enhanced after the introduction of NCQDs. The selectivity shows a volcano-type trend toward the molar ratio of gas products (CO/H2), indicating the vital role of NCQDs in the reaction processes. Specifically, the hybrid system with 4 mg NCQDs presents the optimal CO production with a selectivity of about 84 %. The tunable ratio of gas products (1:2 to 4:1) as syngas is favorable for Fischer–Tropsch chemistry. [37] Besides, the selectivity toward CO2 reduction to CO can be intentionally tailored via controlling solvent ratio (MeCN:H2O). It is evident that the dispersity of NCQDs has a great effect on the activity and selectivity (Fig. S15). The excessive addition of dark-colored NCQDs lowers the light intensity through the depth of reaction solution, thus resulting in the decreased activity. For comparison, the photocatalytic activity of CO2 over CQDs/Ru systems was carried out under the identical conditions, and the marginally enhanced photocatalytic performance was observed (Fig. S16a), implying the superiority of NCQDs as efficient light-harvesting antenna. Moreover, it is found that the NCQDs/Ru system was inactive in low content of MeCN and showed limited merits in low concentration of CO2 (Fig. S16b). Therefore, it is reasonably deduced that dissolved CO2 is favorably harnessed in the homogeneous pseudo-supramolecular systems instead of the adsorbed CO2.

The evolution of reaction products as a function of irradiation time was further studied to probe the stability of NCQDs/Ru system. As the reaction proceeded, a stagnation phenomenon of reaction rate occurs concomitantly with prolonged time (Fig. S17), which is similar to the reported photoactive TMCs-based systems. [38] After adding equal amount of fresh Ru complex into the system, the performance can be effectively re-activated. In contrast, the activity was still vanished with the introduction of new NCQDs, indicating the degradation of Ru is the limiting factor of the stability and the non-covalent interactions between NCQDs and Ru are reversible. Additionally, it is found that the reaction solution sustains stable homogeneous transparent states after 8 h irradiation, akin to the states under ambient conditions (inset of Fig. S17 and Fig. S18), demonstrating the stability of NCQDs during the reaction, [39] as consolidated by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement (Fig. S19). Furthermore, mercury poison measurement excludes the role of heterogenous demetallation of Ru complex on the enhanced catalytic performance (Fig. S20). [40]

We next deliberately carried out a series of control experiments to probe the underlying reaction mechanism under otherwise similar conditions. To verify the participation of CO2 as a reactant in the reaction, only H2 production was detected by replacing CO2 with argon (Ar) gas (Fig. 5b), indicating the photostability of NCQDs (Entry 1). [41] The reaction system in the absence of TEOA exhibits trace evolution amount of H2 and CO (Entry 2), suggesting that the photoexcited states of Ru complex are reductively quenched by TEOA. [42] In the dark, there were no detected gas products in the system (Entry 3), suggesting the involvement of truly photoexcited states to initiate the reaction, as further consolidated by the fact that no reaction occurred when the Ru complex was absent (Entries 4 and 5). Control experiments by using cis-[Ru(bpy)2]Cl2 or [Ru(phd)3](PF6)2 instead of Ru complex (Entry 6) showed that no gas products were detected (Fig. S21), indicating the photochemical CO2 reduction is dependent on the excited states of Ru complex. The 13C isotope labeling experiment was implemented to ascertain the carbon sources of photochemical products by mass spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5c, the result indeed confirms the produced CO is originated from the reactant CO2 rather than any other impurities. [43–45] In addition, using nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO) with similar structural merits of NCQDs as typical electron acceptor, the deteriorative CO evolution performance indicates the absence of effective active sites on the N-rGO for CO2 reduction reactions (Fig. S22 and S23), suggesting that the enhanced performance is not ascribed to the electron transfer property of NCQDs.

To gain a deeper insight into the effect of NCQDs on the catalytic selectivity for CO2 reduction over Ru complex, the electrochemical performance was further investigated with a glassy carbon electrode by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in CO2 or Ar saturated acetonitrile solution containing 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBABF4) as supporting electrolyte. [46] For pristine Ru complex, near-identical CV features were documented when the bubbling gas of CO2 was replaced by Ar, revealing that these two electrochemical peaks arise from Ru complex present in the electrolyte solution instead of CO2 related reactions. For comparison, much lower current was observed under identical conditions with only NCQDs or analogues (Fig. S24). Therefore, the significant difference at much negative potential upon exposure to different gases (Fig. 5d) for the NCQDs/Ru assembly indicates the dynamic π–π interactions modulate the ligand circumstance of metal ruthenium center via electronic coupling, leading to the improved selectivity [19,47]. The excited state of metal complex with suitable redox potential as active site is the premise for driving CO2 reduction reaction.

It has been established that metal-based semiconductor quantum dots can facilitate the energy transfer with organic molecules [48–50]. Subsequently, we examined the energy transfer mechanism between metal-free NCQDs and Ru complex under controllable light irradiation conditions. The pure Ru system exhibits an excited state-dependent performance under monochromatic light (λ2) irradiation (Fig. S25a). Unexpectedly, the optimal NCQDs/Ru obeys a similar law and concomitantly exhibits inferior activity under identical conditions. The attenuation could be attributed to the extinction (interior energy transfer) within the molecular energy level orbitals of NCQDs (Fig. S25b), though the intrinsic PL spectra only show minor stokes shift in a certain range. Additionally, no traces of products were detected under 365 nm light irradiation, ruling out the possibility of NCQDs as donor of photoactive electron or radiation energy transfer mechanism for CO2 reduction. Moreover, the monochromatic light experiments with various intensity proceed in a similar fashion, which potentially excludes the effect of antagonistic absorption on the activity (Fig. S25c). Comparatively, the enhanced activity emerges under cutoff light (λ1) irradiation (Fig. S25d), suggesting that the NCQDs as light-harvester under continuous light spectrum is attributed to overall photon harvesting in the NCQDs/Ru system. To further validate the above assumptions, the control activity tests were carried out under dual light source irradiation including cutoff light and monochromatic light. With the assistance of cutoff light irradiation, NCQDs/Ru shows superior performance than blank Ru under different monochromatic light irradiation (Fig. 5 e and S25e). Meanwhile, 365 nm light can be only favorably harvested by means of the photon switching (excitation-dependent) properties of NCQDs. Furthermore, using 365 nm light as monochromatic light, the synergistic effect can be achieved over the NCQDs/Ru only when the cutoff light reaches a certain intensity (Fig. 5f), indicating the interior extinction of NCQDs could be weakened under continuous irradiation spectrum. Therefore, the NCQDs as UV–vis light chromophore can be utilized to effectively enhance overall photon harvesting efficiency and expand light spectrum response range of Ru complex.

To rationally demonstrate the occurrence of energy transfer in NCQDs/Ru system, the effect of Hg2Cl2 as an indicator of the performance of CO2 reduction was deliberately investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 a, a dramatic drop in the activity of the NCQDs/Ru system can be observed when the reaction solution containing a small amount of Hg2Cl2. However, the activity of pure Ru system is enhanced inversely. It is speculated that the Hg2Cl2 molecule can potentially promote the dynamics of CO2 reduction reaction as the active sites and favorably accept the exciton energy from the NCQDs superior to Ru complex in NCQDs/Ru system. Subsequently, we further substantiated the intermolecular energy transfer between NCQDs and Ru using the femtosecond transient absorption (fs-TA) and time-resolved PL decay spectra. Fig. 6 b shows the fs-TA spectra of NCQDs with pump light excitation at 400 nm, probed at 440−700 nm and at indicated delay times from 1 ps to 2 ns. Compared with the ground-state absorption and PL spectra, the NCQDs exhibit differential absorption spectra from 440 to 480 nm as decay time, which could be assigned to the Franck-Condon states [51]. In addition, the positive absorption spectra from 650 to 700 nm are attributed to the excited state absorption (ESA). Moreover, the negative peaks from 480 nm to 650 nm correspond to the signal containing stimulated emission (SE) from a photoexcited state and partial contribution of ground-state bleaching (GSB) [52]. In contrast, the peaks corresponding to NCQDs in the NCQDs/Ru exhibit significant deactivation (Fig. 6c), while the TA spectra of pristine Ru complex (Fig. S26a) feature the unique GSB signal at 450 nm, which indicate the efficient exciton transfer from NCQDs to Ru in the hybrids. Furthermore, by fitting their decay kinetics with exponential function, the NCQDs/Ru shows shorter average decay time than pristine NCQDs while the lifetime of NCQDs/Ru is apparently longer than that of pristine Ru complex (Fig. 6d, S26b, and Table S1), [53] indicating that the simultaneous occurrence of the intersystem crossing from spin-singlet to spin-triplet state of NCQDs and the intermolecular energy transfer of excited triplet states (excitons) from NCQDs to Ru complex, which thus contribute to the augment of favorable long-lived excited states of Ru complex for CO2 photochemical reduction [54,55]. Based on the above experimental evidence and discussion, we can tentatively propose a plausible assembly-promoted energy transfer mechanism for photochemical CO2 reduction, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 6 e. i) As a UV–vis light-harvesting unit, the unique photophysical characteristics of NCQDs result in efficient photon switching and interior exciton transfer owing to the weak extinction under continuous visible spectrum irradiation; ii) the intrinsic structural merits of NCQDs including the planar structure with high graphitization and surface functional groups induce the spatially dynamic and reversible non-covalent π–π and electrostatic interactions with Ru complex, providing effective energy transfer channels; iii) the intermolecular energy transfer of long-lived excited states facilitate the overall utilization of photon over Ru complex via energy transfer mechanism (exciton transfer), combined with its intrinsic photon absorption; and iv) the enhanced overall photon harvesting efficiency results in the increased population of excited states, i.e., electron-hole charge carriers, of Ru complex with modulated electronic structure, thereby promoting the photochemical conversion rate of CO2 reduction to CO despite lacking of the efficient active sites. In this regard, the NCQDs/Ru dyad possesses great potential for CO2 reduction when cooperated with an effective cocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The extended applications of NCQDs/Ru dyad for efficient CO2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para: To further corroborate the role of NCQDs as a versatile promoter on the ameliorated photocatalytic performance for CO2 reduction to CO over Ru complex, we preliminarily investigated the NCQDs/Ru dyad as a chromophore (photosensitizer) for homogenous and heterogenous CO2 reduction systems containing cocatalysts under different test conditions (Table S2). For instance, jointed with the NCQDs/Ru, the cobalt phosphide (CoxP) nanoplates (NPs) were firstly synthesized via the gas-solid reaction as heterogeneous cocatalysts for the photochemical CO2 reduction. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after the phosphorization treatment, CoP phase (JCPDS, 29−0497) and Co2P phase (JCPDS, 32−0306) were observed for the as-prepared CoxP NPs while the Co(OH)2 phase was disappeared, demonstrating the total conversion of Co(OH)2 into CoxP [56,57]. Besides, the CoxP products with rough surface well inherited the two-dimensional morphology of the Co(OH)2 precursors (Fig. 7 b-e). It is found that no product was observed either in the blank CoxP system (Entry 1) or the NCQDs/CoxP system (Entry 2) in Fig. 7f, indicating that the pristine CoxP is non-photoactive and acts as a cocatalyst for the CO2 reduction reaction. The inclusion of NCQDs can enhance the performance of Ru-photosensitized CO2 reduction to CO over the CoxP with a turnover frequency (TOF) of 643 h−1 reaching an apparent quantum yield (AQY) of 0.084 % (Entry 5), which are significantly superior to those of the NCQDs/Ru system (Entry 3) and are almost two-fold of those of blank Ru/CoxP system (Entry 4), comparing to the previously reported Ru-based heterogeneous systems (Fig. 7 h and Table S3). The improved performance could be ascribed to the cascade of energy transfer from NCQDs to Ru and electron transfer from Ru to CoxP cocatalysts during the photocatalytic CO2 reduction processes [58]. Besides, the UV light can be efficiently utilized by NCQDs to enhance the photosensitization efficiency of Ru complex in the heterogeneous system as well (Entry 6). No product was detected without the cutoff-light irradiation (Entry 7), which is similar to the situation of the NCQDs/Ru dyad system.

Subsequently, we investigated the extended applications in the homogenous systems using Co(bpy)3 2+ (denoted as Co) complex as cocatalyst [59]. As shown in Fig. 7g, the raw Co complex (Entry 1) and NCQDs/Co system (Entry 2) exhibited no product for the CO2 reduction processes, suggesting that the Co complex stands out as a role of cocatalyst instead of photosensitizer, unlike the Ru complex. The optimal ternary system delivered a TOF of 1200 h−1 with an AQY of 0.13 % for the CO2 reduction to CO (Entry 5), which is almost 9 and 2-times higher respectively than those for the NCQDs/Ru system (Entry 3) and the Ru/Co system (Entry 4) under identical conditions, much higher than most of the previous Ru-based homogeneous systems reported so far (Fig. 7 h and Table S4). Compared with the heterogeneous system, the UV light can notably promote the performance of the optimal system in the homogeneous system under the cutoff-light illumination (Entry 6) and is inoperative alone as well (Entry 7). The above experimental results imply the general applicability of the dynamic assembly-promoted energy transfer mechanism for the photocatalytic CO2 reduction systems with homogenous and heterogeneous cocatalysts.

In summary, a novel pseudo-supramolecular system was constructed by assembling metal-free NCQDs (∼2 nm) with Ru complex at a molecular scale without the necessity of any complicated synthetic procedures. The fs-TA in conjunction with time-resolved PL measurements demonstrate that the effective dynamic and reversible non-covalent interactions and proper energy matching among the two components facilitate the unidirectional exciton transfer from NCQDs to Ru complex, coupled with N doping-induced intramolecular exciton transfer in NCQDs, which synergistically enhance the overall photon utilization efficiency via the energy transfer mechanism. As a result, the as-assembled NCQDs/Ru dyad systems exhibit superior catalytic performance for visible-light-driven CO2 reduction to CO in homogeneous and heterogeneous systems, as compared to pristine Ru systems. Moreover, under cutoff visible light irradiation, UV light can be effectively utilized to further enhance the photon-harvesting efficiency of Ru complex by means of photon switching properties of NCQDs. The concept of pseudo-supramolecular assembly described herein may provide new insights into the maneuver of mono(multi)nuclear transition metal complex systems with advanced light-harnessing antenna in the realm of supramolecular chemistry for photoredox-catalyzed applications and beyond.

Shao-Hai Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ming-Yu Qi: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Yun-Yan Fan: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Ye Yang: Writing - review & editing, Resources. Masakazu Anpo: Writing - review & editing. Yoichi M.A. Yamada: Writing - review & editing. Zi-Rong Tang: Writing - review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Yi-Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (22072023, 21872029, U1463204, 21173045), the Program for National Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talents, the Natural Science Foundation (NSF) of Fujian Province for Distinguished Young Investigator Rolling Grant (2017J07002), NSF of Fujian Province (2019J0106), the Program for Leading Talents of Fujian Universities, and the 1st Program of Fujian Province for Top Creative Young Talents is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2021.120157.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
